perturbation.original_text: (c) "MS Licensed Materials" means collectively, to the extent owned by a member of the MS Provider Group, any hardware settings and configurations, generic software libraries and routines, and generic document templates not separately commercialized by the MS Provider Group (as defined below) and used by MSCI prior to the Trigger Date. For the avoidance of doubt, the MS Licensed Materials does not include (i) any patent, trademark or service mark of the MS Provider Group, or (ii) any infrastructure hardware or software (e.g., monitoring software and systems, customized operating systems (and components such as AFS, DNS, AD, etc.), and middleware). For the avoidance of doubt, the document templates do not include any references to members of the MS Provider Group or its personnel.
perturbation.changed_text: (c) "MS Licensed Materials" means collectively, to the extent used by MSCI prior to the Trigger Date, any hardware settings and configurations, generic software libraries and routines, and generic document templates. For the avoidance of doubt, the MS Licensed Materials does not include (i) any patent, trademark or service mark, or (ii) any infrastructure hardware or software. For the avoidance of doubt, the document templates do not include any references to personnel from any group.
perturbation.explanation: The change removes the phrase "to the extent owned by a member of the MS Provider Group" and "not separately commercialized by the MS Provider Group (as defined below)". This introduces an in-text contradiction because Section 2.1 states "MS Grant. MS hereby grants (subject to any existing third party contractual obligations) to MSCI...", which implies that MS can only grant rights to materials they have the right to grant, usually through ownership. By removing the ownership clause in the definition, it's now unclear if the MS Licensed Materials include materials that MS doesn't own, creating a conflict with Section 2.1 regarding the scope of the license.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.1 MS Grant. MS hereby grants (subject to any existing third party contractual obligations) to MSCI a non-exclusive, perpetual, irrevocable, world- wide, royalty-free license for MSCI to use, modify, copy, create derivative works of and sublicense, for any business purpose, the MS Licensed Materials.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the phrase "to the extent owned by a member of the MS Provider Group" from the definition of "MS Licensed Materials". This creates a contradiction with Section 2.1, which states "MS hereby grants (subject to any existing third party contractual obligations) to MSCI...", implying that MS can only grant rights to materials they own. By removing the ownership clause, it is unclear whether the 'MS Licensed Materials' include materials MS does not own, directly conflicting with the granting clause in Section 2.1 and potentially granting rights MS does not have.

perturbation.original_text: 5.  LIMITATIONS OF LIABILITY (a) MSCI agrees that neither MS nor its affiliates or subsidiaries (other than MSCI) (collectively, the "MS Provider Group") and the respective directors, officers, agents, and employees of the MS Provider Group shall have any liability, whether direct or indirect, in contract or tort or otherwise, to MSCI for or in connection with this Agreement or the transactions contemplated hereby or any actions or inactions by or on behalf of the MS Provider Group in connection with this Agreement and such transactions.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.  LIMITATIONS OF LIABILITY (a) MSCI agrees that neither MS nor its affiliates or subsidiaries (other than MSCI) (collectively, the "MS Provider Group") and the respective directors, officers, agents, and employees of the MS Provider Group shall have any liability, whether direct or indirect, except for gross negligence or willful misconduct, in contract or tort or otherwise, to MSCI for or in connection with this Agreement or the transactions contemplated hereby or any actions or inactions by or on behalf of the MS Provider Group in connection with this Agreement and such transactions.
perturbation.explanation: The insertion of "except for gross negligence or willful misconduct" into Section 5(a) creates an in-text contradiction. The original text provides a blanket limitation of liability for the MS Provider Group. The changed text now introduces an exception where liability *does* exist if there's gross negligence or willful misconduct. This conflicts with the initially stated absolute limitation of liability, creating uncertainty about the extent to which MS is shielded from liability and creates an in-text contradiction. It also affects 5(c).
perturbation.contradicted_text: (c) Notwithstanding the provisions of Section 5(a) and (b), none of the members of the MS Provider Group and the MSCI Provider Group shall be liable for any special, indirect, incidental, consequential or punitive damages of any kind whatsoever in any way due to, resulting from or arising in connection with the performance of or failure to perform MS's or MSCI's obligations under this Agreement. This disclaimer applies without limitation (i) to claims for lost profits, (ii) regardless of the form of action, whether in contract, tort (including negligence), strict liability, or otherwise, and (iii) regardless of whether such damages are foreseeable or whether any member of the MS Provider Group or the MSCI Provider Group has been advised of the possibility of such damages.
perturbation.justification: The original text in Section 5(a) provides a blanket limitation of liability for the MS Provider Group. The changed text introduces an exception for 'gross negligence or willful misconduct'. This directly contradicts the 'Notwithstanding the provisions of Section 5(a)' clause in the Contradicted Text, Section 5(c), as 5(c) assumes a complete limitation of liability based on the original 5(a). The exception now introduces a case where the MS Provider Group *can* be liable, which creates a contradiction with the premise upon which section 5(c) is founded.

perturbation.original_text: 6.6 Amendments and Waiver. (a) Any provision of this Agreement may be amended or waived if, but only if, such amendment or waiver is in writing and is signed, in the case of an amendment, by each party to this Agreement, or in the case of a waiver, by the party against whom the waiver is to be effective.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.6 Amendments and Waiver. (a) Any provision of this Agreement may be amended by mutual agreement in writing signed by both parties. Waivers, however, can be granted unilaterally by either party, and such waiver need not be in writing to be effective.
perturbation.explanation: The modification to Section 6.6(a) creates an in-text contradiction regarding the requirements for waivers. The original text states that any waiver must be in writing and signed by the party against whom the waiver is to be effective. The altered text indicates that waivers can be granted unilaterally by either party and need not be in writing. This creates a direct conflict about whether a written, signed document is required for a valid waiver, introducing uncertainty about enforcement of the agreement and creates ambiguity.
perturbation.contradicted_text: (b) No failure or delay by any party in exercising any right, power or privilege hereunder shall operate as a waiver thereof nor shall any single or partial exercise thereof preclude any other or further exercise thereof or the exercise of any other right, power or privilege. The rights and remedies herein provided shall be cumulative and not exclusive of any rights or remedies provided by law.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 6.6(a) introduces a direct contradiction with the original intent and potential enforcement of waiver provisions as outlined in Section 6.6(b). The original Section 6.6(a) required all waivers to be in writing and signed, ensuring a clear record and mutual agreement. The altered Section 6.6(a) now allows unilateral, non-written waivers. This directly contradicts the purpose of Section 6.6(b), which seeks to prevent implied waivers based on inaction or delay, and implies a written formal waiver. By allowing for unwritten waivers, the modified text significantly weakens the enforceability and clarity that Section 6.6(b) attempts to establish, creating a clear inconsistency and potential dispute point.
